replies.author: @Davinadevina02
replies.text:
The country may be warm, have friendly people, and beautiful scenery, but if people believe the government is failing them economically, they will want to leave in search of better opportunities so they can earn enough money to survive.

Years ago, the British pound and the Malawi kwacha were much closer in value, but over time the kwacha has lost significant value. This devaluation has contributed to inflation, making everyday goods and services much more expensive. As a result, not everyone in Malawi has access to a decent-paying job. Many people rely on farming as their main source of income, often just to meet their basic needs.

Higher education has also become more expensive, including at public universities. Even after graduating, many young people struggle to find employment because there are not enough jobs to match the growing number of graduates. At the same time, the population is growing faster than the economy, making it even more difficult to create enough employment opportunities and improve living standards.
